text: The surgeon toggles between standard and immunofluorescence imaging to assess the anatomy, noting that the cystic duct appears dilated and is not highlighted by the ICG, which was administered earlier.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the cystic duct, using toggling techniques to achieve the critical view of safety. Despite the use of immunofluorescence imaging, the cystic duct and biliary tree remain unlit by the indocyanine green (ICG) dye.

The surgeon attempts to achieve the critical view of safety by teasing down the cystic duct and performing short cautery pumps on crossing vessels. The duct is milked upwards, and the surgeon decides to truncate it, noting the absence of stones within the duct.
The surgeon isolates the cystic artery using robotic instruments, preparing for further dissection in the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon inspects the cystic duct area for stones but does not observe any visible stones.
The surgeon carefully dissects the cystic duct from the cystic plate, using precise movements to separate the duct from the surrounding tissue.
